sentences.text: My name is Yakob Basner.
Yakob, Y-A-K-O-B.
Basner, B-A-S-N-E-R.
Yes.
No.
There was a name some people call me, Yasha.
I-- I was born in the 8th of December, 1927.
In a few days, it will be 68.
I was born in Riga, Latvia.
Well, our family consisted of five.
Except myself, there was Father, Leizer.
He was born in 1905.
Mother, Guta, was born in 1902.
And a twin brother and sister, they were born Abe and Tsilya.
They were born in 1934, 18 of July.
It was a working family.
Father was working.
Mother was a housewife.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] It was a traditional Jewish family with a kosher kitchen, kosher house.
And-- [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] --I attended-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I attended a Yiddish-- Yiddish school.
Mhm.
It was actually not Yiddish.
It was a Hebrew, and the beginning.
I attended many schools during my life.
But the first one was a Hebrew school.
And the name was [HEBREW], what means Torah and respect.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And we lived in a Jewish neighborhood.
The landlord was also a Jew, [?
Milbitzki. ?] And the house where we lived, there was about 47 apartments. 45 was occupied by Jewish people.
Only two were Gentiles.
He was-- father was a leather cutter and designer, also a machine operator for shoes.
In the beginning-- in the years he worked for a shop, [LATVIAN], in the old-- in the downtown of Riga.
Later, he had his own workshop.
There were different schools in Riga.
We had about 25 of them, Jewish schools, but with different languages were taught.
There were private schools.
There were public schools, schools with Hebrew language, schools with Yiddish language, also a Latvian school, a Russian school, a German school, other technical school.
Even a school who prepare nurses for the hospital of [HEBREW].
And in the schools, the main language was, let's say, if it-- it was Hebrew, where I attended.
We also were learning the Latvian language.
And at home, it was Yiddish.
It--
Yeah, it depends what kind of school.
There were schools who were-- the public schools, the Yiddish schools, they had some religious, but very, very little religious subjects.
In our school, we had [HEBREW], the Bible.
We had [HEBREW], we had Gemara even.
And the Jewish rules and law.
So every day, we had approximately two lessons was religious lessons.
We went-- yes, at the shul-- we had a lot of shuls in Riga.
Talking about the Riga community, what we had, there were approximately 50,000 Jewish in Riga.
It means about 5% of the population.
And-- but it was such-- it was a unique and wide developed Jewish community with different kinds of organizations, institutions, schools, clubs.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Whatever it's-- you can name only for-- for a Jew-- for a community though.
It was one for-- one of the most educated communities in the world.
Jewish communities in the world.
And-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] --about shuls.
Shuls, we had-- we had four shuls with choirs.
We called them [HEBREW].
And 10 temples or synagogues or shuls, as we called them, without choirs, regular shuls.
And many, many shuls, little shuls, some of them only for the High Holidays.
But some of them were functioning all year long.
But they were the small ones.
We attended the shul on [INAUDIBLE], on Stabu iela.
And the shul was called [?
Zelen ?] [?
Shul. ?] The rabbi was Rabbi Kilov.
And they had a choir.
It was a nice shul.
If there was a choir, it was-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] it-- you need a membership.
You have to pay for the High Holidays.
And it was a more-- a bigger-- bigger, actually, bigger shul.
Singing, yeah.
Boys, not girls.
Boys.
A boy choir.
No.
No girls.
We didn't have Reform in Judaism yet.
If we are talking about the political structure of Riga, of Latvia at all, we had-- when I was born, it was a democratic republic with a saeima or a parliament.
It called the saeima.
And there were Jewish representatives, three of them.

And there were different kind of organizations in that time, several left-wing organizations, [NON-ENGLISH], or middle-wing, or right-wing Zionist organizations, different kind of-- such organization as the-- the People Party, National Democratic Party, Agudat Yisrael, and different kind of other organizations.
And they have representatives in the saeima.
It was still 1934. 1934, power took a dictator, Karlis Ulmanis.
He was-- he was from the Farmer or [?
Patient ?] party.
And he took over power as-- he liquidate the saeima.
There wasn't more.
And there wasn't-- they were closed.
All the left-wing organizations became illegal.
They became more antisemitic.
And he tried to-- he tried to concentrate the industry and trade stores to be in the Latvian's hands.
So some Jewish people would sell their stores or their plants or factories to-- or to the government or to Latvians.
And some of them leaved Latvia, Riga.
One of them was a candy factory, a big one, Lima.
They went to Israel.
And now it's a famous factory till now, in Israel.
Tell us a little bit about-- well, did your father's job change at that time?
Did he have any restrictions on him, or did anything change after it became a dictatorship?
There were-- there were strictly-- more strictly, the rules.
You couldn't-- you couldn't own a workshop without to have a license, a special license.
And Father went to study.
And he got a license.
And he was able to have the workshop.
It was for everyone.
But in this trade, by shoes, most-- most of the shoemakers or-- were Jewish people.
We had, also, a lot of-- a lot-- in the 1940s, there stays-- still were the Zionist organizations, we had different kinds of this, Betar, Harzalia.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And there were still working Yiddish clubs.
There's a cultural club, Yiddish, even tennis club, [INAUDIBLE].
In Riga, where we prepared, even a marine club who prepared the first sailors for Israel.
They were prepared in-- in Riga.
Bought a boat.
And they trained on the boat.
Different kind of-- In the Yiddish club that we had, it was a whole building.
And there was a regular basic working theater, Yiddish theater.
And we had visitors from abroad.
This famous Habima came to Riga, or visit us as-- for the main role-- Michalesko, Witler, Nina Talina, and many, many more.
Yes, partly.
I was a child yet.
But I participate, yes.
I personally learned about antisemitic-- antisemitism when I was five years old.
I was sick and taken to the hospital, to the children hospital.
And I didn't know yet the Latvian language.
And the nurse, she got mad that I don't know what she-- she was talking to me.
And she screamed in Latvian, [SPEAKING LATVIAN]!
Well, it means if you don't know the Latvian language, you have to go to Palestine.
I didn't understand what she means.
And when Mother came to visit me, I told her.
And she explain to me.
So for one side, it was a good lesson.
Because I was trying all my life to go to Palestine, to Israel.
From the other side, I felt the first time in my life, what it means, antisemitism, to be a Jew, at five years old.
But antisemitism was everywhere.
You could feel it even when the people-- Jewish people weren't-- they didn't work in the government.
The whole police, we had one policeman, a Jew.
From-- if we were about 5% Jewish population, only 0.2% were busy in the government.
And not only in the government, but in some institutions of the government, a very small part.
And you could feel it everywhere.
If you had to go to the police station, or-- or everywhere, you could feel.
Only one policeman was a Jew.
Yes.
We didn't have-- we have only one-- well, actually, our house was a traditional Jewish house.
And for the holidays, Mother prepared everything.
I told you already, it was kosher.
And she prepared for [? every ?] every holiday.
And we felt it very, very, very much, that we have the holidays.
Every holiday, it was-- In-- in general, in Riga, you could feel-- on the street, even-- that it's a Jewish holiday.
All the Jewish stores were closed.
People would go to the synagogue, to the shuls.
And you felt it very, very much, that it is a holiday.
Yes.
So in 19-- in 1940, 17 of June, the Russians occupied Latvia.
Actually, they came as liberators.
But as we know, it was-- they were occupants.
And it changed.
The whole life changed.
All the Hebrew schools were closed.
It was forbidden, the Hebrew language.
All the Zionist organizations were closed.
There was still-- it still remained, Yiddish schools.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Jewish-- the people were naive.
And they-- they were thinking that everything is-- it is-- was written.
And they believed that now they'll be equal with all the others.
But around the end of the year, in June, beginning of June, 19-- 1941, 12,000 Jewish people were expropriated and expelled from Riga to Siberia.
Only 4,000 of them survived.
They came back after the war, after World War II.
Some of them-- some of them stayed there, even till the-- in the '50s.
Some of them came back in '53 and '56, even.
Because a lot of them got sentences.
They were in prison or in-- in labor camps, forced labor camps.
Some of them lived in cities or villages, worked hard.
And a lot of them didn't survive.
Organizations were closed.
Hebrew schools were closed.
Only Yiddish schools remained.
Hebrew schools were closed.
Zionist organizations were closed.
That was the restrictions.
And all the stores, factories were nationalized, or taken away from the owners and-- by the government.
And the people who owned it were sent to Siberia.
Father's work, father's-- he liquidate his-- his workshop.
And he went to a factory, to work, a factory, as a leather cutter.
Yes.
It was owned by the government.
We-- yes.
We had-- we had-- in Riga, we had many Jewish-Yiddish newspapers, magazines, and so on.
And I was reading them and know the situation in Europe.
And except that we had refugees from Germany, from Poland.
And even refugees from Finland, when the war was going on with Russia in 1939, they came to Riga, some of them, from Vyborg, from Finland.
But in our house, we had refugees from Germany.
And we supported them and helped them.
And some of them would go, they make [?
[NORWEGIAN]. ?] They would get married.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Married only on the paper, and went to Israel.
Some of them remained in Riga.
They married Jewish girls from Riga.
And then they went to another country, to Sweden, to other countries, to Belgian.
And from there, they went to Israel.
They would go to Israel.
No, they were in the-- in the same building.
In the same house where we lived.
I knew them and I contacted with them.
And--
Yes.
We knew what was going on.
But nobody knew exactly.
They didn't know what-- what will be the end, what will be the solution of the Jewish problem.
In 19-- 19-- --'41, 22nd of June, the war began.
And it went so quickly, we want to go-- to evacuate, to go to Russia.
But people would come back and say that the border is closed, and you need a special paper to cross the border.
And also, the Latvians already began to shoot.
And people get killed.
And we came back and-- at home-- in our home.
And we-- we stayed in Riga.
But after a week, the 1st of July, the Germans were already in Riga.
Yes, yes.
Yes, we tried.
But we went not too far.
Because we met over there one of the uncles, my-- father's-- father's sister's husband, with the family of my-- my uncle and aunt, they returned.
They came back because they couldn't make it.
So we decide so-- so there is no way to go.
And it was, very quickly, only a weekend, they were there.
We felt, already, in the first days when they came, the Latvians welcomed them with music, flowers.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And already after a few days, two days, they were organized from the Latvian organization, Perkonkrusts.
It was an organization similar to the Nazi organization in Germany.
It was the-- the organization, the Perkonkrusts, was forbidden by Karlis Ulmanis in 1934.
But the members of the organization still remained there.
And when the Germans came, the first days, they organized.
They helped the members who were there, and the new members came.
And they organized auxiliary police, or helps police.
And they tried, by them self, to solve the Jewish problems already, in the first days.
The Germans were-- it was OK for-- to the Germans because they didn't have to do the-- the dirty job.
And they came to our house, to-- to every-- every Jewish house, took the Jewish people.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Some of them were taken to the central or main jail.
And from the jail, to Bikernieki Forest, where they were killed.
Some of them, directly to Bikernieki Forest.
They came, also, to our home-- to our house.
And directed by our-- by-- by the only family-- of the two families who live-- who resided in our house, one was a family with children, and we grew up together.
He knew Yiddish.
He was as a friend of ours.
No.
He was Latvian.
And he directed the auxiliary police where the Jewish-- where the Jewish live.
And even when somebody hide, as my father, he told, it couldn't be.
He has to be here.
And he was looking all around.
And some of them-- he didn't find my father.
But others he found, and was taken away.
In the first days, most of the men from our house were taken away to the main or central jail.
Also, my friend-- my best friend, Yakob, also, [INAUDIBLE] was taken.
My uncle, Gutman Feld, and many, many others, friend and neighbors.
My father was hidden.
But they couldn't leave-- they couldn't leave the apartment now, well, not to take somebody.
So he was looking to me, you know?
I was 13 years old.
My bar mitzvah was when the Russians were in Riga.
It was the last simcha, the last-- the last holidays, what we celebrated, or last celebration in our house.
And so he looked at me and asked different questions to ridicule me.
And he didn't know what to do.
So he took Mother.
He took my mother.
But they didn't take her to the-- to the jail, but took her to the headquarters of the police in Riga, on Aspazijas Bulvaris.
And there she witnessed what was going on there.
They were drunk, celebrating there.
They ridiculed the Jewish people who were there, beating them up.
There was also taken, our landlord, Milbitzki.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] They stripped him naked, put on his head a Russian Army hat.
And he had to sing Russian songs in front of all of them.
Girls were raped.
There was a cry-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] --going on.
It was terrible.
Yes.
Yes.
